segments.text: A piece of perforated glass
Poverty
Troubled by something
Joe Thompson to his wife
The silence
"They flash upon that inward eye"
To voice his protest
The roofs were so crowded with people that they appeared to be heaving and swaying.
Those who were blessed by the love of God
Empathy
Hercules
Balthazar
She wanted to make sure that Antonio would not bleed to death
Not to be trusted
Portia, by Shylock
She had to return to Padua that night
Portia refers to Bassanio as 'my lord'. He is her newly wedded husband whom she deeply loves and respects.

Bassanio had gone to Venice to help his dear friend Antonio, who was in grave danger.

He left in such haste because Antonio had written an urgent letter saying that the bond with Shylock had been forfeited - all his ships had been lost and Shylock was demanding his pound of flesh. Bassanio felt it was his duty to stand by his friend in this crisis.

The 'secret vow' Portia speaks of is actually a clever pretence. She tells Lorenzo that she has vowed to live a life of prayer and contemplation until Bassanio returns. However, this is not her real intention. She is actually planning to disguise herself as a young lawyer and travel to Venice to save Antonio's life in court. She uses this vow as a cover story so no one suspects her true plan.

Portia asks Lorenzo to take charge of her household and manage all affairs at Belmont in her absence. She trusts him completely as a close friend of Bassanio. Later, Portia instructs her servant Balthasar to travel with great speed to her cousin Dr. Bellario in Padua, bring back whatever letters and clothing Bellario sends, and meet her at the ferry to Venice without delay.

From her conversation with Nerissa, we learn that Portia's real intention is to go to Venice disguised as a young lawyer to defend Antonio in court. She reveals that both she and Nerissa will dress as young men, intending to use her intelligence and knowledge of the law to save Antonio. The businesslike Portia of this scene is more admirable because she takes decisive action in a crisis rather than simply waiting. She shows great intelligence, courage, and loyalty - qualities that make her one of Shakespeare's most remarkable characters.

The 'principal' Shylock asks for is the 3,000 ducats he had lent to Antonio - the original sum of money, without interest. Portia refuses because the bond only entitled Shylock to a pound of flesh. Since Shylock insisted on the bond and refused all earlier offers of mercy and money, the law now holds him strictly to the exact terms of the bond - nothing more and nothing less.

The 'forfeiture' refers to the pound of flesh nearest Antonio's heart, as stated in the bond. The danger Shylock would face is that the bond specifies flesh alone - he must cut exactly one pound with no blood shed. If he sheds even a drop of blood or cuts more or less than a pound, his lands and goods would be seized by the state and his life would be forfeit.

Under Venetian law, if any alien directly or indirectly attempts to take the life of a citizen, half his property goes to the intended victim, the other half to the state, and his life is at the mercy of the Duke. Since Shylock is considered an alien and plotted against the life of Antonio (a citizen), this law applies to him fully.

Antonio offers to let Shylock keep half of his goods (which would otherwise go to Antonio under the law), on the condition that Shylock converts to Christianity and makes a will leaving all his remaining property to his daughter Jessica and her husband Lorenzo upon his death.

Shylock is in a hurry to leave because he says he is unwell and not feeling well enough to stay. He is a broken man - stripped of his money, his daughter, his religion, and his dignity all at once. The trial has utterly crushed him.

Shylock can be considerably blamed for the outcome of the trial because he stubbornly refused every offer of mercy and money, insisting on his 'pound of flesh' even when offered far more than the original sum. His rigid insistence on the literal terms of the bond ultimately gave Portia the legal opening to destroy him completely.

The children's mother died after collapsing on the doorstep of her home. She had gone out, possibly to get some drink or food, and fell down and died on the street. The factors that led to her death were poverty, neglect, and most likely excessive drinking. She had lived a wretched, miserable life without any proper care, support, or medical attention, and her body finally gave out.

Before her death, the villagers largely ignored and looked down upon the woman. She was considered a disgrace - a drunkard and an outcast. Nobody helped her or showed her kindness during her lifetime.

After her death, their attitude changed dramatically. They showed concern - not truly for her sake, but for the children she left behind. This behaviour reveals that human nature is often hypocritical and self-serving. People are more comfortable helping those who cannot answer back, and death often brings out a false sense of generosity.

John - a strong, healthy boy who had been working and was capable of looking after himself.

Kate - an older girl, described as bold and self-assertive, who had already been mixed up in bad company.

Maggie (Margaret) - the youngest child, who had fallen from a window as a baby and was left permanently disabled, unable to walk.

John was taken in by a farmer who needed a boy to work on his farm. Kate was taken in by a woman from the village who thought she could make use of the girl.

Maggie was left behind as no one wanted to take a crippled, helpless child - until Joe Thompson agreed to take her home temporarily.

Maggie is the 'Angel in Disguise'. The word 'disguise' refers to the fact that she appears to be a burden - a helpless, crippled child whom no one wants. Yet beneath this helpless exterior, she is truly a blessing and a gift.

Her arrival completely transforms Joe Thompson's home. His wife, who had been cold and somewhat bitter (as the couple had no children of their own), gradually softens as she cares for Maggie. The child's innocence, gentleness, and vulnerability awaken deep maternal love in Mrs. Thompson's heart. The home is filled with warmth, tenderness, and a new sense of purpose. Maggie, who seemed to be nothing but a burden, turns out to be the greatest gift the childless couple could have received.

Margot is a nine-year-old girl who, unlike the other children, was born on Earth and moved to Venus only five years ago. The author describes her as a very pale, fragile, thin girl who looks as though she had been washed out - like a faded photograph. She is withdrawn and quiet, and unlike the other children, she actually remembers the sun clearly from her years on Earth. She had even written a poem about it. She longs desperately to return to Earth and to feel sunlight again.

'They' are the other children in Margot's class - children who were born on Venus and have lived there all their lives. They live on Venus, which in Bradbury's story is a world of constant, unrelenting rain and jungle. One reality of this planet is that the sun appears for only one hour every seven years, after which the rains return and continue without pause for another seven years.

Two words to describe what the children were experiencing: guilt and shame.

The mention of Margot's name affects them this way because they had cruelly locked her in a closet before going out to enjoy the one hour of sunshine - and in their excitement, they forgot all about her. Now, as the rain returns, they suddenly remember what they have done. They realise they have robbed Margot of the one thing she lived for - and the weight of that cruelty hits them all at once.

The children had eagerly awaited the appearance of the sun, which was due to come out for one hour that day after seven years of continuous rain. This event was special because it happened only once every seven years, making it an extraordinarily rare and precious occurrence.

This event meant far more to Margot because she actually remembered the sun from her life on Earth. She had felt its warmth and seen its brightness. For the other children, the sun was almost an abstract idea, but for Margot it was a longed-for connection to her former life and happiness.

The central theme of 'All Summer in a Day' is the cruelty of jealousy and exclusion, and the terrible consequences of bullying. It also explores themes of longing, isolation, and the loss of something irreplaceable.

The important lesson the children have learned is that cruelty has consequences that cannot be undone. Their jealousy of Margot led them to lock her away, and now they must live with the guilt of having robbed her of seven more years of waiting.

The story ends on a note of hope because the children, overwhelmed by guilt and shame, are seen walking slowly toward the closet to let Margot out. This suggests the possibility of remorse, growth, and perhaps even compassion. They cannot undo the harm, but their willingness to face her suggests a moral awakening.

The words 'wandered' and 'lonely' convey a mood of aimlessness, melancholy, and isolation. The poet is drifting without purpose, feeling cut off from the world around him, much like a cloud floating alone in the sky.

His mood changed when he suddenly came upon a magnificent, unexpected sight - a vast field of golden daffodils growing beside a lake, beneath the trees, swaying and dancing in the breeze. This beautiful, joyful vision lifted him instantly out of his loneliness.

Wordsworth describes the sight as overwhelming and breathtaking. He saw a huge crowd - a 'host' - of golden daffodils stretched out along the shore of a lake, nestled beneath the trees. The flowers were not still but alive with movement, fluttering and dancing in the breeze as though they were joyful beings. The sheer number and vitality of the flowers made it an unforgettable spectacle.

Wordsworth compares the daffodils to the stars that shine and twinkle in the Milky Way. He says the daffodils, like stars, are countless and spread out in a long, glittering stretch across the landscape.

This comparison is appropriate because just as stars seem to go on endlessly across the night sky, the daffodils seemed to stretch without end along the margin of the bay. Both stars and daffodils also seem to twinkle and dance - one with light, the other with movement - creating a similar feeling of wonder and delight.

When Wordsworth says 'Ten thousand saw I at a glance', he means that the daffodils were so vast in number that even a single sweeping glance could take in thousands of them. It is a hyperbole used to emphasise the enormous scale and abundance of the flowers.

Two other words in the given extract that create the impression of large numbers are 'crowd' and 'host' - both suggesting a great multitude of flowers gathered together.

The immediate effect of seeing the daffodils was one of delight and joy. Wordsworth watched them with great pleasure, though at the time he did not fully realise how deeply the sight had affected him.

In the long term, the daffodils became a source of lasting happiness. Whenever he lay on his couch in a vacant or pensive mood, the image of the daffodils would flash upon his 'inward eye' - his imagination - and fill his heart with joy, as if the flowers were dancing for him again. This poem tells us that Wordsworth had a deep, almost spiritual connection with Nature. He believed Nature was not merely a backdrop but a living source of healing, comfort, and joy for the human soul. Even a single encounter with natural beauty could nourish the spirit for years to come.

In the opening lines, Angelou describes the free bird as a creature of joy, liberty, and ease. The free bird leaps on the back of the wind, floats downstream, and dips its wings in the orange sun rays. It claims the sky as its own and has the freedom to think of another breeze - its world is entirely open, pleasurable, and full of natural beauty and movement.

The caged bird's physical condition is one of complete restraint and suffering. Its wings are clipped so it cannot fly, and its feet are tied so it cannot even move freely within its cage. It is a prisoner with no hope of escape.

Mentally, the caged bird is in deep anguish. It stands on the 'grave of dreams' - all its hopes and desires are dead or buried. Its very shadow seems to shout in the darkness of a nightmare. It is trapped in fear, despair, and helplessness. The only outlet it has is its voice, and so it opens its throat to sing.

The phrase 'grave of dreams' means that all the hopes, aspirations, and desires of the caged bird have been killed and buried. A grave is where something dead is put to rest permanently - so the phrase suggests that the bird's dreams are not merely unfulfilled but completely destroyed, with no possibility of revival. The caged bird sings of freedom - it sings about the things it can never have: open skies, the wind, the sun, and the right to live without chains. Its song is a cry of longing and protest against its imprisonment.

The free bird represents white people (or any privileged group) who enjoy all the rights, freedoms, and opportunities that society offers. The caged bird represents Black people of America (or any oppressed group) who are denied basic freedoms and are trapped by systems of racism, discrimination, and injustice. One group of people that could be called 'caged birds' in today's world: refugees who are forced to live in camps or detention centres, stripped of their freedom and unable to return home or build a new life.

The title tells us that Maya Angelou herself experienced the life of a 'caged bird'. She grew up as a Black woman in racially segregated America and faced poverty, trauma, racism, and oppression. The title suggests that she personally understands the pain and longing that drives a caged bird to sing - because she has lived it. Her poetry and writing became her own 'song' - her way of voicing protest, pain, and the desperate longing for freedom.

Two ways in which the world of the caged bird differs from that of the free bird:

1. The free bird moves through an open, boundless world of sky and wind, while the caged bird is confined to a narrow cage with clipped wings and tied feet - it cannot move at all.

2. The free bird's thoughts are of pleasure and adventure (another breeze, the orange sun), while the caged bird's thoughts are of nightmares and dead dreams - its inner world is defined by fear and grief rather than hope and joy.
